title: Dockerfile Stage 2 binary URL and path not validated before template injection
intent: Generated Dockerfiles never contain malformed or shell-unsafe content from binary tool config. URL scheme must be https:// and destPath must be a safe absolute path.
description: src/build/docker/dockerfile.ts:81-82 interpolates binary.url and binary.destPath directly into Dockerfile content via template strings: 'RUN curl -fsSL "${binary.url}" -o "${binary.destPath}"'. There is no validation that: (1) binary.url is a valid https:// URL before insertion, (2) binary.destPath is a safe absolute path with no newlines, quotes, or shell metacharacters. A malformed URL causes a confusing Docker build failure. A path with embedded newlines could inject additional Dockerfile instructions. The Stage2Config type accepts any string values. Steps: (1) add validateBinaryUrl(url: string): void helper that throws if url is not a valid https:// URL (use new URL(url) + check protocol); (2) add validateBinaryDestPath(path: string): void that throws if path contains newlines, quotes, or backslashes; (3) call both before inserting into Dockerfile lines; (4) call validation in the Stage 2 generator and/or in the tool manifest layer where binaries are added; (5) run docker/ tests to confirm no regression. Scope: ~15 lines in one file.
groomingNotes: SECURITY UPGRADE — elevated from 'should' to 'must'. This is an active Dockerfile injection vulnerability: destPath with embedded newlines can inject arbitrary Dockerfile instructions into the Stage 2 build. CQ-031 (tool install) is already shipped so the attack surface is live. For a platform whose core promise is 'security by default', shipping a Dockerfile injection vector is directly contradictory. The fix is ~15 lines and low-risk — no reason to defer. Grooming notes unchanged: validateBinaryUrl() + validateBinaryDestPath() in dockerfile.ts:81-82, called before template insertion. No dependencies. Run docker/ tests after.

groomingNotes: PRIORITY UPGRADED: should → must. Same classification as BUG-029 (Dockerfile injection) and BUG-035 (approval queue silent drop) — all three are cases where a silent failure directly violates a core security promise. For this item: trust-modes/switch.ts:70-75 and commands/queue.ts:63-68 MUST THROW (security-critical path — caller must know the state change failed); heartbeat/reporter.ts:56-61 and fleet/discovery.ts:60-65 log-only (best-effort, non-critical). title: approve-action tool saveQueue() writes approval queue without error handling
intent: Approval queue writes never fail silently. A disk write error in saveQueue() must be caught and logged — a silently dropped write means an approval is lost, directly violating the no-auto-send guarantee.
description: src/design/tools/approve-action.ts:59 defines a saveQueue() function that calls writeFileSync(QUEUE_FILE, JSON.stringify(queue, null, 2)) without try-catch. This is an agent-side script installed into the deployed agent's tools directory. If the file write fails (disk full, permission error), the approval is silently dropped — the user receives a Telegram approval notification but their approve/reject action is never persisted. The no-auto-send guarantee depends on this write succeeding. Note: this is a GENERATED file. The fix must be applied in the template/generator that produces it, not in the output file directly. Steps: (1) locate the generator for approve-action.ts in src/design/tools/ (likely tools.ts or approve-action generator); (2) update the saveQueue() template to wrap writeFileSync in try-catch with console.error(err) and process.exit(1) on failure (the tool is a CLI script — failure must be loud); (3) verify the fix propagates to newly generated tools by running design/tools/ tests; (4) if existing deployed approve-action.ts files are not regenerated automatically, note in the fix that re-running 'clawhq init' or 'clawhq build' regenerates the tool. Scope: 1 generator file, ~5 lines added to template.
groomingNotes: PRIORITY UPGRADED: 'should' → 'must'. The no-auto-send guarantee is a core product promise — it appears in marketing, README, and product docs. A silently dropped saveQueue() write means the user receives a Telegram approval notification but their approve/reject action is lost and never persisted. The agent then proceeds (or doesn't) with no record of what the user chose. This is a direct violation of the safety contract, not a quality-of-life fix. The fix is in the generator that produces approve-action.ts — wrap writeFileSync in try-catch with console.error + process.exit(1) (loud failure is correct for a CLI tool). Existing deployed agents get the fix on next 'clawhq build' Stage 2 rebuild. No blocking dependencies — run design/tools/ tests to verify generated output. Sprint alongside CQ-005 or immediately after.

title: Path traversal risk in web dashboard /api/init deployDir input

title: parseInt/parseFloat NaN propagation in CLI commands
intent: All numeric CLI options must reject non-numeric input with a clear error message. NaN must never propagate silently into cron schedules, interval timers, or config fields.

title: Web dashboard CDN resources lack Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes
intent: CDN-hosted assets in web/layout.tsx must carry SRI integrity attributes. A compromised CDN cannot inject malicious code into the ClawHQ dashboard.
description: src/web/layout.tsx lines 37 and 39 load Pico CSS from cdn.jsdelivr.net and htmx from unpkg.com without SRI integrity= attributes. If either CDN serves a malicious version of these assets, any code runs in the user's browser with access to the dashboard. This is particularly sensitive for a product whose core promise is data sovereignty and security by default. The dashboard runs on localhost:3737 but it controls agent config, credentials, and approvals. Steps: (1) compute SHA-384 integrity hash for Pico CSS v2 (documented on picocss.com); (2) compute SHA-384 integrity hash for htmx 2.0.4 (documented on htmx.org); (3) add integrity='sha384-...' crossorigin='anonymous' to both <link> and <script> elements in layout.tsx; (4) run web/ tests and verify dashboard still loads. Scope: 1 file, 2 element attribute additions. Medium effort — hash values must be looked up from official sources.
groomingNotes: SECURITY: This is a must-priority fix given ClawHQ's sovereignty promise. The dashboard controls agent config, approval queues, and credentials — CDN-supplied JS without SRI is a single CDN compromise away from running arbitrary code in that context. Fix requires looking up published SRI hashes (do NOT compute from a local download — use the official CDN SRI published by each project). (1) Pico CSS v2: visit https://picocss.com/docs/cdn — the CDN link section provides the integrity hash for the version in use; (2) htmx 2.0.4: the official htmx CDN reference at https://htmx.org/docs/#installing provides the integrity hash for each release; (3) update src/web/layout.tsx line 37 (<link> for Pico CSS) and line 39 (<script> for htmx) to add integrity='sha384-...' crossorigin='anonymous'; (4) run web/ tests; (5) open the dashboard in a browser to confirm assets still load. NOTE: If SRI hashes cannot be retrieved (no web access), add TODO comment and log as a known gap rather than skipping.

title: Silent .catch(() => {}) in sanitizer.ts swallows audit log and quarantine write errors
intent: No security event in the sanitizer module is silently discarded. Both audit log write failures and quarantine write failures must log a warning. A sanitizer that silently fails to log threats is indistinguishable from one that has no threats.
description: src/secure/sanitizer/sanitizer.ts lines 125 and 130 use fire-and-forget .catch(() => {}) on writeAuditLog() and writeQuarantine() calls. This silently swallows errors from audit log and quarantine file writes with zero observability. If writes fail (disk full, permissions, path errors), the sanitizer continues as if nothing happened — threat events are never logged and injected content is never quarantined, with no trace. The audit logger (BUG-015) sweep fixed silent catches in audit/logger.ts, but missed these two callers in sanitizer.ts. Pattern: identical to BUG-012 through BUG-019 (add console.warn, keep fallback behavior). Steps: (1) open src/secure/sanitizer/sanitizer.ts; (2) replace .catch(() => {}) on line 125 with .catch(err => console.warn('[sanitizer] audit log write failed:', err)); (3) replace .catch(() => {}) on line 130 with .catch(err => console.warn('[sanitizer] quarantine write failed:', err)); (4) run secure/ tests. Scope: 2 one-line changes in one file. Zero behavior change — fire-and-forget is correct, just needs to log.
groomingNotes: 2 one-line changes in one file. sanitizer.ts:125 — replace .catch(() => {}) with .catch(err => console.warn('[sanitizer] audit log write failed:', err)). sanitizer.ts:130 — replace .catch(() => {}) with .catch(err => console.warn('[sanitizer] quarantine write failed:', err)). Fire-and-forget is the correct pattern (sanitize() must never block on logging) — only the error visibility is wrong. Pattern identical to BUG-012 through BUG-019 and BUG-015 (which fixed audit/logger.ts but missed these callers). No blocking dependencies. Run secure/ tests after. Zero-risk.
